does: This paper introduces RMB, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating reward models used in LLM alignment. The benchmark spans over 49 real-world scenarios and implements both pairwise and Best-of-N evaluation protocols to measure how effectively reward models guide alignment optimization. The authors establish that their benchmark correlates positively with downstream alignment task performance, demonstrating that RMB evaluations meaningfully predict how well reward models will perform in actual alignment training. Through extensive empirical analysis, they reveal previously undiscovered generalization defects in state-of-the-art reward models and highlight the promise of generative reward models as an alternative approach.
connects: This work addresses limitations in existing reward model evaluation methods that fail to capture alignment performance directly. Prior research has developed various reward models and alignment techniques, but evaluation has relied on limited distributions and metrics disconnected from actual alignment objectives. The paper builds on the broader line of work in RLHF and preference-based LLM alignment, extending beyond simple accuracy metrics toward evaluation schemes that directly reflect downstream task performance. It responds to the recognized gap between reward model evaluation and real-world alignment effectiveness, combining insights from reward learning literature with comprehensive empirical assessment across diverse scenarios.
enables: The benchmark enables future research to systematically evaluate reward models against meaningful alignment criteria, allowing researchers to identify which models generalize better across real-world settings. By revealing generalization defects and highlighting generative reward models' potential, this work opens avenues for developing more robust reward modeling approaches. The correlation between benchmark performance and downstream alignment tasks provides a foundation for using RMB as a standard evaluation tool during development. This positions researchers to build better reward models with predictable alignment performance and creates opportunities for investigating reward model architectures, training procedures, and majority voting strategies that improve generalization beyond current state-of-the-art approaches.
